title: Ransomware & Service Disruption Awareness
description: Test your understanding of ransomware risks, service disruption, reporting, and response steps for local government employees.

questions.prompt: Why can ransomware be especially damaging for local governments?

choices.text: Because it only affects personal devices
Because it can disrupt public services, internal operations, and public trust
Because governments never use backups
Because ransomware only targets federal agencies

questions.explanation: Ransomware can interrupt government services and create operational and public trust problems.

questions.prompt: What did the Ohio Golf Manor example reinforce?

choices.text: That backups are always safe during a ransomware attack
That small local governments can suffer serious ransomware disruption, including loss of backups
That ransomware only affects police departments
That ransom payments always restore systems

questions.explanation: The Golf Manor example shows that even small local governments can face major disruption and backup loss.

questions.prompt: Which of the following is a common way ransomware gets into an organization?

choices.text: Phishing emails or malicious attachments
Approved payroll processing by itself
Reading a printed memo
Using a government website homepage

questions.explanation: Phishing and malicious attachments are common ransomware entry points.

questions.prompt: True or False: If a suspicious ransomware screen appears, employees should try to fix the system quietly before reporting it.

choices.text: True
False

questions.explanation: Suspected ransomware should be reported immediately, not handled quietly by the employee.

questions.prompt: Scenario: A clerk notices that several shared folders suddenly cannot be opened and one computer shows a ransom note. What is the best first response?

choices.text: Delete the ransom note and continue working
Report the incident immediately and follow agency instructions, including isolation if directed
Transfer affected files to a personal laptop
Wait until the end of the day to see if the problem clears up

questions.explanation: Quick reporting and following agency instructions are the correct first steps.

questions.prompt: Why are backups alone not enough?

choices.text: Because backups never help at all
Because backups may also be encrypted, deleted, or unavailable if not properly protected
Because only state agencies may use backups
Because continuity planning is unrelated to ransomware

questions.explanation: Backups must be protected, tested, and separated well enough to be useful during recovery.

questions.prompt: Scenario: A supervisor says not to report a suspected ransomware event yet because it might create panic. What is the best response?

choices.text: Delay reporting until leadership feels ready
Follow the agency's reporting process anyway because delay can increase damage
Post online to warn the public first
Delete any evidence to keep the issue private

questions.explanation: Delaying reporting can let ransomware spread and make recovery harder.

questions.prompt: Which outside partners may be involved in a ransomware response?

choices.text: OCIC, Auditor of State, FBI, and IC3
Only the office break room manager
Only the internet provider
No outside reporting is useful

questions.explanation: State and federal partners may all play a role depending on the incident and agency procedures.

questions.prompt: What is the U.S. Joint Ransomware Task Force?

choices.text: A local government procurement group
An interagency effort co-chaired by the FBI and CISA to combat ransomware
A private insurance program
A software patching schedule

questions.explanation: The JRTF is a U.S. government interagency effort led by the FBI and CISA to coordinate against ransomware.

questions.prompt: Scenario: During a ransomware incident, someone suggests paying immediately because that is the fastest way back online. Which statement is most accurate?

choices.text: Payment always guarantees successful recovery
Federal guidance says paying does not guarantee data recovery and may encourage more attacks
Any employee can approve payment during an emergency
Backups become unnecessary once payment is made

questions.explanation: The FBI does not support paying a ransom because it does not guarantee recovery and may encourage future attacks.

questions.prompt: Which of the following may be an early warning sign of ransomware?

choices.text: Shared drives suddenly becoming unavailable
Strange file extensions or ransom notes
Unexpected account or security alerts
All of the above

questions.explanation: All of these can be signs that ransomware or related malicious activity is underway.

questions.prompt: Scenario: Your office is recovering from ransomware, and another employee says backups are enough so no continuity planning is needed. What is the best response?

choices.text: Agree, because recovery planning ends with backups
Explain that continuity planning is still needed to keep critical services functioning during recovery
Ignore the issue because only IT needs to care
Move all recovery files to a personal account

questions.explanation: Backups matter, but agencies also need continuity planning so essential services can continue during recovery.
